questions.questionText: The rate-limiting enzyme of de novo purine synthesis is:
questions.optionB: Glutamine-PRPP amidotransferase (GPAT)
questions.explanation: De novo purine synthesis: PRPP + glutamine → phosphoribosylamine (by GPAT — committed step). Inhibited by: AMP, GMP, IMP (feedback). Activated by: PRPP. Purine ring is built on ribose-5-phosphate. Sources of atoms: glycine (C4,C5,N7), glutamine (N3,N9), aspartate (N1), CO2 (C6), N10-formyl-THF (C2,C8). IMP is the first complete purine nucleotide → AMP or GMP. Reference: Harper's — Purine Synthesis.

questions.questionText: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome (HGPRT deficiency) causes hyperuricemia AND devastating neurological manifestations. The neurological symptoms (self-mutilation, dystonia) are paradoxically NOT due to uric acid because:
questions.optionA: Uric acid crosses the BBB easily
questions.optionB: Allopurinol (lowering uric acid) controls gout but does NOT improve neurological symptoms — the CNS dysfunction results from impaired purine SALVAGE → depleted intracellular GTP/GMP pools in developing neurons → disrupted dopaminergic neurotransmission in basal ganglia
questions.explanation: Lesch-Nyhan — neurobiology: HGPRT (hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase): salvages hypoxanthine→IMP and guanine→GMP using PRPP. Deficiency → PRPP accumulates → feeds de novo purine synthesis → OVERPRODUCTION of purines → hyperuricemia → gout (responds to allopurinol). NEUROLOGICAL (does NOT respond to allopurinol): loss of purine salvage → depleted intracellular GTP in DEVELOPING basal ganglia neurons → impaired GTP-dependent processes: (1) GTP→dopamine signaling (GTP-cyclohydrolase I → BH4 → tyrosine hydroxylase cofactor → dopamine synthesis impaired), (2) GTP-binding proteins, (3) RNA/DNA synthesis in developing neurons. Dopamine deficiency in caudate/putamen → self-injurious behavior (lip/finger biting), dystonia, choreoathetosis. X-linked recessive. Reference: Harper's — Lesch-Nyhan Neurology.

questions.questionText: Gout pathophysiology involves monosodium urate (MSU) crystal deposition. The specific mechanism triggering the acute inflammatory response is:
questions.optionB: MSU crystals are phagocytosed by macrophages → activate NLRP3 inflammasome → caspase-1 activation → IL-1β release → neutrophil recruitment → acute inflammatory response; colchicine works by inhibiting tubulin polymerization preventing neutrophil migration and NLRP3 activation
questions.explanation: Gout — molecular inflammation: MSU crystals form when serum urate >6.8 mg/dL (supersaturation): (1) MSU crystals phagocytosed by tissue macrophages, (2) Crystals activate NLRP3 INFLAMMASOME (cytoplasmic multi-protein complex), (3) NLRP3 → ASC → pro-caspase-1 → CASPASE-1 (active), (4) Caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1β → IL-1β (active, potent pro-inflammatory cytokine), (5) IL-1β → neutrophil recruitment → acute gouty arthritis. First MTP joint predilection: cooler temperature → lower urate solubility → crystal deposition. Treatment targets: Colchicine: inhibits TUBULIN polymerization → impairs neutrophil migration, microtubule-dependent NLRP3 assembly, and phagocytosis. Anakinra: IL-1 receptor antagonist. NSAIDs: reduce inflammation. Allopurinol/febuxostat: chronic — lower urate production. Probenecid: increase renal excretion. Reference: Harper's — Gout Inflammasome.
questions.conceptTag: Gout MSU NLRP3 inflammasome IL-1beta colchicine

questions.questionText: The rate-limiting enzyme of de novo pyrimidine synthesis is:
questions.optionB: CPS-II (carbamoyl phosphate synthetase II, cytoplasmic)
questions.explanation: De novo pyrimidine synthesis: CPS-II (rate-limiting, cytoplasmic) — glutamine + CO2 + 2ATP → carbamoyl phosphate. Different from CPS-I (mitochondrial, urea cycle). Pyrimidine ring is synthesized first then attached to ribose (opposite to purines). In mammals, first 3 enzymes (CPS-II + ATCase + DHOase) are on a single trifunctional protein: CAD. UMP → UTP → CTP. Feedback inhibition: UTP inhibits CPS-II. Reference: Harper's — Pyrimidine Synthesis.

questions.questionText: Adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency causes:
questions.optionB: Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID)
questions.explanation: ADA deficiency → deoxyadenosine accumulates → dATP accumulates → inhibits ribonucleotide reductase → blocks DNA synthesis in ALL cells, especially lymphocytes (rapidly dividing) → SCID (both T and B cell deficiency). Most common cause of autosomal recessive SCID. Purine nucleoside phosphorylase (PNP) deficiency → primarily T cell deficiency. Treatment: PEG-ADA enzyme replacement, gene therapy (first approved gene therapy), bone marrow transplant. Reference: Harper's — ADA Deficiency.

questions.questionText: 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) acts as an anticancer drug by inhibiting:
questions.optionB: Thymidylate synthase (irreversible — suicide inhibitor)
questions.explanation: 5-FU: pyrimidine analog → converted to 5-FdUMP (fluorodeoxyuridine monophosphate) → forms stable covalent complex with thymidylate synthase and N5,N10-methylene-THF → irreversibly inhibits thymidylate synthase (suicide inhibition) → blocks dTMP synthesis → 'thymineless death' → inhibits DNA synthesis. Used in: colorectal, breast, head & neck cancers. Leucovorin (folinic acid) enhances 5-FU efficacy. Reference: Harper's — 5-FU.

questions.questionText: Hereditary orotic aciduria is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: UMP synthase (orotate phosphoribosyltransferase + OMP decarboxylase)
questions.explanation: Hereditary orotic aciduria: AR, deficiency of UMP synthase (bifunctional enzyme: OPRT + OMP decarboxylase) → cannot convert orotic acid to UMP → orotic acid accumulates in urine. Features: megaloblastic anemia (not responsive to B12/folate, responds to uridine), growth retardation, orotic acid crystals in urine. Treatment: oral uridine (bypasses the block). Also elevated orotic acid in OTC deficiency (carbamoyl phosphate diverted). Reference: Harper's — Orotic Aciduria.

questions.questionText: Ribonucleotide reductase converts:
questions.optionB: Ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides (for DNA synthesis)
questions.explanation: Ribonucleotide reductase (RNR): catalyzes reduction of ribonucleoside diphosphates (NDP) → deoxyribonucleoside diphosphates (dNDP). Essential for DNA synthesis. Converts: ADP→dADP, GDP→dGDP, CDP→dCDP, UDP→dUDP. Requires thioredoxin/glutaredoxin as electron donors. Allosterically regulated: ATP activates, dATP inhibits (ADA deficiency → dATP accumulation → inhibits RNR). Hydroxyurea inhibits RNR (used in sickle cell disease, CML). Reference: Harper's — RNR.

questions.questionText: Allopurinol paradoxically worsens orotic aciduria in hereditary orotic aciduria. This occurs because:
questions.optionB: Oxypurinol (allopurinol metabolite via XO) is converted to oxypurinol ribotide by OPRTase → inhibits OPRTase (the FIRST step of UMP synthase bifunctional enzyme) → blocks orotic acid → OMP conversion → MORE orotic acid accumulates
questions.explanation: Allopurinol and orotic acid — interaction: Allopurinol: structural analog of hypoxanthine. XO converts allopurinol → OXYPURINOL (alloxanthine, the active metabolite that inhibits XO). Additional interaction: Oxypurinol can serve as substrate for OPRT (orotate phosphoribosyltransferase, first activity of UMP synthase) → oxypurinol ribonucleotide. This product INHIBITS OPRTase itself. In hereditary orotic aciduria (UMP synthase deficiency → both OPRTase and OMP decarboxylase impaired → orotic acid accumulates): allopurinol would further inhibit the already-deficient OPRTase → MORE orotic acid accumulation → WORSENS condition. Treatment for hereditary orotic aciduria: URIDINE (bypasses the block completely). Note: OTC deficiency also causes orotic aciduria (different mechanism — CP spillover) — allopurinol interaction is less relevant there. Reference: Harper's — Allopurinol Orotic Acid.
questions.conceptTag: Allopurinol oxypurinol OPRTase orotic aciduria interaction

questions.questionText: The purine salvage pathway enzyme APRT (adenine phosphoribosyltransferase) converts:
questions.optionB: Adenine + PRPP → AMP
questions.explanation: Purine salvage: recycles free purine bases → nucleotides (energy-efficient alternative to de novo synthesis). APRT: adenine + PRPP → AMP. HGPRT: hypoxanthine + PRPP → IMP, and guanine + PRPP → GMP. Salvage is especially important in brain and RBCs (cannot perform de novo synthesis). Salvage pathway defects: HGPRT deficiency → Lesch-Nyhan. APRT deficiency → 2,8-dihydroxyadenine kidney stones. Reference: Harper's — Purine Salvage.

questions.questionText: Uric acid is the end product of purine catabolism in humans. Most mammals have uricase (urate oxidase) which converts uric acid to a more soluble product. Recombinant uricase (rasburicase) is used clinically to:
questions.optionB: Rapidly convert uric acid → ALLANTOIN (200x more soluble, easily excreted) for emergency treatment of tumor lysis syndrome and severe treatment-refractory gout — it achieves faster urate reduction than allopurinol/febuxostat
questions.explanation: Rasburicase — clinical uricase: Humans: lost uricase gene (pseudogene) → uric acid is END product of purine metabolism. Most mammals: uricase converts urate → ALLANTOIN (200x more water-soluble → easily excreted). Rasburicase (recombinant Aspergillus uricase): RAPID uric acid reduction (within hours vs days for allopurinol). Uses: (1) TUMOR LYSIS SYNDROME (TLS): massive cell death (chemotherapy of leukemia, lymphoma) → release of nucleic acids → purine catabolism → hyperuricemia → urate nephropathy, AKI. Rasburicase: rapid urate clearance → prevents renal damage. (2) Pegloticase (PEGylated uricase): for treatment-refractory chronic tophaceous gout. CONTRAINDICATION: G6PD deficiency — rasburicase generates H2O2 as byproduct → hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients. Lab: rasburicase continues working in blood tube → falsely low uric acid unless kept on ice. Reference: Harper's — Rasburicase.
questions.conceptTag: Rasburicase uricase allantoin TLS G6PD contraindication

questions.questionText: Azathioprine is an immunosuppressant prodrug that is converted to:
questions.optionB: 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP, a purine analog that inhibits de novo purine synthesis)
questions.explanation: Azathioprine → 6-MP (in vivo) → 6-thio-IMP (by HGPRT) → inhibits de novo purine synthesis (feedback inhibition) and is incorporated into DNA → immunosuppression (inhibits lymphocyte proliferation). Used in: organ transplant rejection prevention, autoimmune diseases (SLE, IBD, RA). 6-MP is also metabolized by XO → allopurinol interaction (reduce 6-MP dose 75%). TPMT (thiopurine methyltransferase) polymorphism affects toxicity. Reference: Harper's — Azathioprine/6-MP.

questions.questionText: Orotic aciduria due to UMP synthase deficiency classically presents with megaloblastic anemia and:
questions.optionB: No response to folate/B12 alone
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. UMP synthase deficiency causes pyrimidine synthesis defect with megaloblastic anemia unresponsive to folate/B12 and elevated urinary orotic acid; treated with uridine.

questions.questionText: Allopurinol lowers uric acid mainly by inhibiting:
questions.optionB: HGPRT
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Allopurinol (and oxypurinol) inhibit xanthine oxidase, reducing conversion of hypoxanthine/xanthine to uric acid.

questions.questionText: Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase (XO). Besides lowering uric acid, it has an important drug interaction with azathioprine/6-MP because:
questions.optionB: XO also catabolizes 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP, active metabolite of azathioprine) → allopurinol blocks 6-MP degradation → 6-MP levels increase 3-5 fold → SEVERE myelosuppression unless dose is reduced to 25-33% of normal
questions.optionD: They compete for the same transporter
questions.explanation: Allopurinol-azathioprine interaction — pharmacology: Azathioprine → converted to 6-MP (liver, non-enzymatic glutathione conjugation). 6-MP metabolism: (1) TPMT (thiopurine methyltransferase): methylation → inactive metabolites, (2) XO (xanthine oxidase): oxidation → 6-thiouric acid (inactive, excreted), (3) HGPRT: conversion to 6-thioGMP → 6-thioGTP → incorporated into DNA → cytotoxicity (therapeutic effect). Allopurinol BLOCKS XO → (1) 6-MP cannot be degraded by XO → plasma levels increase 3-5x, (2) More 6-MP shunted through HGPRT → MORE cytotoxic metabolites. Result: severe MYELOSUPPRESSION (pancytopenia) if dose not adjusted. MANDATORY: reduce azathioprine/6-MP dose to 25-33% when co-administered with allopurinol. TPMT deficiency further increases risk. Febuxostat: also XO inhibitor → same interaction. Reference: Harper's — Allopurinol Drug Interactions.

questions.questionText: Tumor lysis syndrome is characterized by:
questions.optionA: Low uric acid and low potassium
questions.optionB: Hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hypocalcemia (from rapid lysis of tumor cells)
questions.explanation: Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS): metabolic emergency from rapid destruction of tumor cells (especially after chemotherapy of highly proliferative cancers — lymphomas, leukemias). Massive release of: (1) Purines → degraded to uric acid → hyperuricemia → uric acid nephropathy/renal failure, (2) Potassium → hyperkalemia → cardiac arrhythmias, (3) Phosphate → hyperphosphatemia → binds calcium → secondary HYPOcalcemia → tetany/seizures. Prevention: rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase — converts uric acid to allantoin), allopurinol, aggressive IV hydration. Reference: Harper's — TLS.

questions.questionText: Thymidylate synthase converts dUMP to dTMP using which folate cofactor?
questions.optionB: N5,N10-methylene-THF (donates both the one-carbon unit and reducing equivalents)
questions.explanation: Thymidylate synthase: dUMP + N5,N10-methylene-THF → dTMP + DHF. Unique reaction: the folate cofactor donates BOTH the one-carbon unit AND the reducing equivalents (unlike other folate-dependent reactions). THF is oxidized to DHF → must be regenerated by DHFR (dihydrofolate reductase). Methotrexate inhibits DHFR → depletes THF → blocks dTMP synthesis → impairs DNA synthesis. 5-FU: suicide inhibitor of thymidylate synthase (after conversion to 5-FdUMP, it forms irreversible ternary complex with enzyme and N5,N10-methylene-THF). Reference: Harper's — dTMP Synthesis.

questions.questionText: CPS-I (carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I) differs from CPS-II in that CPS-I:
questions.optionA: Is cytoplasmic and used for pyrimidine synthesis
questions.optionB: Is mitochondrial, uses NH3 as nitrogen source, requires NAG activation, and functions in the urea cycle
questions.explanation: CPS-I vs CPS-II comparison: CPS-I: MITOCHONDRIAL, uses free NH3 (ammonia), requires N-acetylglutamate (NAG) as allosteric activator, functions in UREA CYCLE (only in liver), rate-limiting step of urea cycle. CPS-II: CYTOPLASMIC, uses glutamine amide nitrogen, no NAG requirement, functions in PYRIMIDINE synthesis (first step of de novo pyrimidine pathway), present in all tissues. This distinction is extremely high-yield for NEET PG. OTC (ornithine transcarbamylase) also mitochondrial — uses carbamoyl phosphate from CPS-I. Reference: Harper's — CPS-I vs CPS-II.

questions.questionText: Ribonucleotide reductase converts:
questions.optionB: Ribonucleoside diphosphates (NDPs) to deoxyribonucleoside diphosphates (dNDPs) — essential for DNA synthesis
questions.explanation: Ribonucleotide reductase (RNR): catalyzes the ONLY pathway for de novo synthesis of deoxyribonucleotides (needed for DNA synthesis). Reduces the 2'-OH of ribose to 2'-H: ADP → dADP, GDP → dGDP, CDP → dCDP, UDP → dUDP. Uses thioredoxin/glutaredoxin as electron donors. Allosteric regulation ensures balanced dNTP pools. Hydroxyurea: inhibits RNR (used in sickle cell disease — increases HbF, and in cancer chemotherapy). Reference: Harper's — Ribonucleotide Reductase.

questions.questionText: Mycophenolate mofetil (immunosuppressant) inhibits:
questions.optionB: Inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH — rate-limiting step in de novo GMP synthesis)
questions.explanation: Mycophenolate (MMF/MPA): potent, selective, reversible inhibitor of IMPDH (inosine 5'-monophosphate dehydrogenase) → blocks IMP → XMP → GMP pathway → depletes guanine nucleotides. Lymphocytes are uniquely dependent on de novo purine synthesis (lack efficient salvage pathway) → selectively immunosuppressive. Used in: organ transplant rejection prevention, lupus nephritis, other autoimmune diseases. IMPDH: IMP + NAD+ → XMP + NADH (rate-limiting for GMP synthesis). Reference: Harper's — Mycophenolate Mechanism.

questions.questionText: The end product of pyrimidine catabolism in humans is:
questions.optionB: β-Alanine (from cytosine/uracil) and β-aminoisobutyrate (from thymine) — both water-soluble, excreted in urine
questions.explanation: Pyrimidine catabolism: Ring is opened and degraded completely (unlike purines). Cytosine → uracil → dihydrouracil → β-ureidopropionate → β-alanine + NH3 + CO2. Thymine → dihydrothymine → β-ureidoisobutyrate → β-aminoisobutyrate + NH3 + CO2. Products are water-soluble → easily excreted (NO precipitation/stone formation, unlike uric acid from purines). 5-FU toxicity: blocked pyrimidine catabolism in patients with dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency → severe 5-FU toxicity. Reference: Harper's — Pyrimidine Catabolism.

questions.questionText: Azathioprine is an immunosuppressant prodrug that is converted to:
questions.optionB: 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP — inhibits de novo purine synthesis at multiple steps)
questions.explanation: Azathioprine: prodrug that is cleaved by glutathione S-transferase to 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). 6-MP is converted by HGPRT to thioinosinic acid (6-thio-IMP) → inhibits de novo purine synthesis at multiple steps (inhibits PRPP amidotransferase and interconversion reactions IMP→AMP, IMP→GMP). Used in: organ transplant immunosuppression, IBD, autoimmune hepatitis. TPMT (thiopurine methyltransferase) metabolizes 6-MP → TPMT deficiency causes severe myelosuppression with standard doses (pharmacogenomic testing recommended). Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase → decreases 6-MP clearance → dose reduction needed. Reference: Harper's — Azathioprine/6-MP.

questions.questionText: The rate-limiting step of de novo purine synthesis is catalyzed by:
questions.optionB: PRPP amidotransferase (glutamine-PRPP amidotransferase — commits PRPP to purine pathway)
questions.explanation: PRPP amidotransferase (glutamine phosphoribosylpyrophosphate amidotransferase): committed step. PRPP + glutamine → 5-phosphoribosylamine + glutamate + PPi. Activated by: PRPP (substrate availability). Inhibited by: AMP, GMP, IMP (end-product feedback inhibition — both adenine and guanine nucleotides must be present for full inhibition). Note: PRPP synthetase (makes PRPP) is also important but not the committed step of purine synthesis specifically. 6-MP and azaserine inhibit this pathway at multiple points. Reference: Harper's — De Novo Purine Synthesis.

questions.questionText: Methotrexate acts as an anticancer drug by inhibiting:
questions.optionB: Dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR — depletes THF → blocks dTMP and purine synthesis)
questions.explanation: Methotrexate (MTX): structural analog of folic acid, competitively inhibits DHFR with ~1000x higher affinity than DHF → prevents reduction of DHF to THF → depletes THF pools → blocks: (1) dTMP synthesis (thymidylate synthase requires N5,N10-methylene-THF), (2) purine synthesis (requires N10-formyl-THF for C2 and C8). Result: impaired DNA synthesis → cytotoxic to rapidly dividing cells. Used in: cancers (leukemia, lymphoma, choriocarcinoma), autoimmune diseases (RA, psoriasis — low dose). Rescue: leucovorin (folinic acid, 5-formyl-THF — bypasses DHFR block). Reference: Harper's — Methotrexate.

questions.questionText: The first pyrimidine nucleotide synthesized de novo is:
questions.optionB: UMP (orotidine 5'-monophosphate → UMP by OMP decarboxylase)
questions.explanation: De novo pyrimidine synthesis: ring is assembled FIRST as a free base (unlike purines where ring is built on ribose-P). Steps: glutamine + CO2 + ATP → carbamoyl phosphate (CPS-II) → + aspartate → carbamoyl aspartate (ATCase) → dihydroorotate → orotate → OMP (orotate + PRPP, by orotate phosphoribosyltransferase) → UMP (OMP decarboxylase). UMP is the FIRST pyrimidine nucleotide. UMP → UDP → UTP → CTP (CTP synthetase, using glutamine). dTMP is made from dUMP (by thymidylate synthase). Reference: Harper's — De Novo Pyrimidine Pathway.

questions.questionText: Febuxostat differs from allopurinol in that febuxostat:
questions.optionB: Is a non-purine selective xanthine oxidase inhibitor (does not require activation by xanthine oxidase)
questions.explanation: Febuxostat: non-purine, selective xanthine oxidase (XO) inhibitor. Unlike allopurinol (purine analog, suicide substrate), febuxostat: (1) does NOT need to be activated by XO first, (2) inhibits both oxidized and reduced forms of XO, (3) more potent urate-lowering, (4) can be used in mild-moderate renal impairment (hepatic metabolism). Both reduce uric acid production. Uricosuric agents (probenecid, benzbromarone): increase renal uric acid excretion (different mechanism). Rasburicase: recombinant urate oxidase (degrades uric acid to allantoin — used in TLS). Reference: Harper's — Febuxostat vs Allopurinol.

questions.questionText: Hydroxyurea is used in sickle cell disease because it:
questions.optionB: Increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production (by stimulating γ-globin gene expression) and inhibits ribonucleotide reductase
questions.explanation: Hydroxyurea in sickle cell disease: (1) Increases HbF (α2γ2) production → HbF interferes with HbS polymerization → reduces sickling frequency. Mechanism of HbF induction: not fully clear but involves NO-mediated activation of γ-globin gene. (2) Inhibits ribonucleotide reductase → reduces WBC and platelet counts (decreases vaso-occlusive events). (3) Generates NO → vasodilation. (4) Increases RBC hydration. HbF levels of 20-30% significantly reduce complications. Also used as anticancer agent (CML, essential thrombocythemia). Reference: Harper's — Hydroxyurea in SCD.

questions.questionText: CPS-II (cytoplasmic) and CPS-I (mitochondrial) have distinct roles. The key regulatory difference is:
questions.optionA: Both are activated by the same effectors
questions.optionB: CPS-I (urea cycle, mitochondrial) requires N-ACETYLGLUTAMATE (NAG) as an essential allosteric activator, while CPS-II (pyrimidine synthesis, cytoplasmic) is inhibited by UTP (feedback) and activated by ATP and PRPP — reflecting their different metabolic roles
questions.explanation: CPS-I vs CPS-II comparison: CPS-I: MITOCHONDRIAL. Reaction: NH3 + CO2 + 2ATP → carbamoyl phosphate (for UREA CYCLE). Activator: NAG (N-acetylglutamate, essential allosteric). Uses FREE AMMONIA. CPS-II: CYTOPLASMIC. Reaction: glutamine + CO2 + 2ATP → carbamoyl phosphate (for PYRIMIDINE SYNTHESIS). Uses GLUTAMINE as nitrogen donor. Regulation: FEEDBACK INHIBITED by UTP (end product); ACTIVATED by ATP (energy status), PRPP (purine availability → coordinate purine and pyrimidine synthesis). In pyrimidine synthesis: CPS-II is RATE-LIMITING in mammals. In bacteria: ATCase (aspartate transcarbamoylase) is rate-limiting → classic allosteric enzyme (CTP inhibits, ATP activates). Key concept: CPS-I and CPS-II use different N donors (NH3 vs glutamine) and are in different compartments — ensuring independent regulation of urea cycle and pyrimidine synthesis. Reference: Harper's — CPS-I vs CPS-II.
questions.conceptTag: CPS-I vs CPS-II NAG UTP regulation compartments

questions.questionText: A patient on 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) chemotherapy develops unexpected severe mucositis, pancytopenia, and neurotoxicity. Testing reveals DPD deficiency. This toxicity occurs because:
questions.optionB: Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) is the rate-limiting enzyme for 5-FU CATABOLISM → deficiency leads to 5-FU accumulation and severe toxicity
questions.optionC: DPD activates 5-FU to its toxic metabolite
questions.optionD: DPD deficiency increases renal clearance of 5-FU
questions.explanation: DPD (dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase) and 5-FU toxicity — pharmacogenomics: DPD is the rate-limiting enzyme for pyrimidine catabolism (NORMAL function: uracil/thymine → dihydrouracil/dihydrothymine). DPD also catabolizes 5-FLUOROURACIL (>80% of administered 5-FU is degraded by DPD in liver). DPD deficiency (DPYD gene variants — 3-5% partial, 0.1% complete): cannot degrade 5-FU → drug accumulates → severe/fatal toxicity (mucositis, diarrhea, myelosuppression, neurotoxicity, hand-foot syndrome). FDA recommends DPYD genotyping BEFORE 5-FU/capecitabine administration. Partial deficiency: dose reduction. Complete deficiency: CONTRAINDICATED. 5-FU mechanism of action: FdUMP (active metabolite) inhibits thymidylate synthase → thymineless death. Reference: Harper's — DPD Deficiency.

questions.questionText: Gemcitabine (2',2'-difluorodeoxycytidine) exerts its antitumor effect primarily by:
questions.optionB: Acting as a nucleoside analog that inhibits ribonucleotide reductase AND incorporates into DNA causing chain termination (masked chain terminator)
questions.optionC: Blocking purine ring synthesis at the first step
questions.explanation: Gemcitabine — dual mechanism: (1) dFdCDP (diphosphate form) INHIBITS ribonucleotide reductase (same target as hydroxyurea) → depletes deoxyribonucleotide pools → impaired DNA synthesis, (2) dFdCTP (triphosphate form) is incorporated into DNA → after one more nucleotide is added, DNA polymerase stalls → 'masked chain termination' (the 3'-terminal position is NOT immediately at the gemcitabine — exonuclease proofreading cannot easily detect and remove it). Also inhibits CTP synthetase (self-potentiating). Used in: pancreatic cancer, NSCLC, bladder cancer, breast cancer. Cytarabine (Ara-C): another nucleoside analog (arabinose sugar) — inhibits DNA polymerase α and incorporates into DNA. Used in AML. Reference: Harper's — Gemcitabine Mechanism.

questions.questionText: TPMT (thiopurine methyltransferase) deficiency is clinically important because patients with this deficiency who receive standard doses of azathioprine/6-mercaptopurine develop:
questions.optionB: Life-threatening myelosuppression due to accumulation of cytotoxic thioguanine nucleotides (6-TGN)
questions.explanation: TPMT pharmacogenomics — critical clinical application: Azathioprine (prodrug) → 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) → three metabolic fates: (1) TPMT methylation → 6-methylmercaptopurine (inactive, detoxification), (2) XO oxidation → 6-thiouric acid (inactive), (3) HGPRT → 6-thioguanine nucleotides (6-TGN, ACTIVE cytotoxic metabolites incorporated into DNA). TPMT deficiency (homozygous ~1 in 300): ALL 6-MP shunted via HGPRT → massive 6-TGN accumulation → severe bone marrow suppression, potentially fatal pancytopenia. TPMT genotyping BEFORE prescribing thiopurines: homozygous deficient → USE ALTERNATIVE or 10% dose, heterozygous → 50% dose reduction. Also important: co-administration with ALLOPURINOL (XO inhibitor) blocks pathway 2 → more 6-MP available → need 75% dose reduction regardless of TPMT status. Reference: Harper's — TPMT Pharmacogenomics.

questions.questionText: Xanthinuria (hereditary xanthine oxidase deficiency) presents with:
questions.optionB: Very LOW serum uric acid with xanthine kidney stones (xanthine is less soluble than uric acid)
questions.optionC: Elevated uric acid in urine only
questions.explanation: Hereditary xanthinuria: autosomal recessive xanthine oxidase (XO) deficiency → cannot convert hypoxanthine → xanthine → uric acid. Results: (1) Very LOW serum and urine uric acid (HYPOuricemia — opposite of gout), (2) Elevated plasma and urine xanthine, (3) Xanthine kidney stones (xanthine is LESS soluble than uric acid at physiological pH). Usually mild/asymptomatic — may present with xanthine stones, myopathy (xanthine deposits in muscle), or be discovered incidentally. Type I: isolated XO deficiency. Type II: combined XO + aldehyde oxidase deficiency (molybdenum cofactor related). Molybdenum cofactor deficiency: most severe — XO + sulfite oxidase + aldehyde oxidase ALL deficient → severe neurological damage (sulfite oxidase deficiency component). Allopurinol mimics this state pharmacologically. Reference: Harper's — Xanthinuria.

questions.questionText: ADA (adenosine deaminase) deficiency causes SCID, whereas PNP (purine nucleoside phosphorylase) deficiency causes primarily T-cell immunodeficiency. The key biochemical difference is:
questions.optionA: ADA deficiency affects only B-cells while PNP affects only T-cells
questions.optionB: ADA deficiency accumulates dATP (toxic to both B and T lymphocytes), while PNP deficiency accumulates dGTP (selectively toxic to T-cells due to their higher dCK activity)
questions.optionC: PNP deficiency causes uric acid stones while ADA does not
questions.optionD: ADA works on pyrimidines while PNP works on purines
questions.explanation: ADA vs PNP deficiency — comparative immunodeficiency: ADA deficiency: adenosine and deoxyadenosine accumulate → dATP accumulates (by deoxycytidine kinase/dCK) → inhibits ribonucleotide reductase → depletes ALL dNTP pools → kills BOTH B and T lymphocytes → SCID (combined immunodeficiency). Treatment: PEG-ADA (enzyme replacement), gene therapy (first FDA-approved gene therapy — Strimvelis). PNP deficiency: inosine and guanosine accumulate → dGTP accumulates → inhibits ribonucleotide reductase primarily in T-CELLS (T-cells have higher dCK activity → accumulate more dGTP than B-cells). Result: predominantly T-CELL immunodeficiency with relatively preserved B-cell function + neurological features (unlike ADA). Both: autosomal recessive. Key principle: toxic deoxynucleotide accumulation. Reference: Harper's — ADA vs PNP.

questions.questionText: Superactivity of PRPP synthetase (gain-of-function mutation) leads to gout because:
questions.optionA: It directly inhibits uric acid excretion
questions.optionB: Excess PRPP accelerates BOTH de novo purine synthesis and salvage pathways → increased purine production → increased uric acid (degradation product of purines)
questions.explanation: PRPP synthetase superactivity — purine overproduction gout: PRPP (5-phosphoribosyl-1-pyrophosphate): critical substrate for BOTH de novo purine synthesis (PRPP amidotransferase — first committed step) and salvage pathways (HGPRT, APRT). PRPP synthetase: ribose-5-phosphate + ATP → PRPP. Superactivity mutations (X-linked) → excessive PRPP → drives purine synthesis above normal needs → excess purines catabolized → hypoxanthine → xanthine → URIC ACID (by xanthine oxidase) → hyperuricemia → gouty arthritis, uric acid nephrolithiasis. Contrast with Lesch-Nyhan (HGPRT deficiency): salvage pathway blocked → PRPP accumulates (NOT used for salvage) → same result — excess de novo synthesis → hyperuricemia + neurological features (self-mutilation, choreoathetosis). Both cause overproduction hyperuricemia. Reference: Harper's — PRPP Synthetase.

questions.questionText: The end product of purine catabolism in humans is uric acid, while the end product of pyrimidine catabolism is:
questions.optionB: β-alanine (from cytosine and uracil) and β-aminoisobutyrate (from thymine) — both are soluble and non-toxic
questions.explanation: Purine vs pyrimidine catabolism — clinical significance: PURINE catabolism → uric acid (insoluble at physiological pH, can cause gout and kidney stones). Humans lack urate oxidase (uricase) → cannot degrade uric acid further (most mammals convert it to allantoin). PYRIMIDINE catabolism → opens the ring → highly soluble, easily excreted products: Cytosine/Uracil: CMP → UMP → uridine → uracil → dihydrouracil (DPD — rate-limiting) → β-ureidopropionate → β-ALANINE + CO2 + NH3. Thymine: thymine → dihydrothymine → β-ureidoisobutyrate → β-AMINOISOBUTYRATE + CO2 + NH3. β-alanine: component of CoA (via pantothenate). β-aminoisobutyrate: elevated in urine during chemotherapy (marker of DNA turnover). Key clinical difference: pyrimidine catabolism is BENIGN (no disease like gout). Reference: Harper's — Pyrimidine Catabolism.

questions.questionText: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) is an immunosuppressant that inhibits:
questions.optionB: IMP dehydrogenase (IMPDH) — the rate-limiting enzyme for de novo GMP synthesis → selectively depletes guanine nucleotides in lymphocytes
questions.explanation: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) — mechanism: MMF is hydrolyzed to mycophenolic acid (MPA) → potent, selective, reversible inhibitor of IMPDH (inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase). IMPDH catalyzes: IMP → XMP (NAD+ required) — rate-limiting step for de novo GMP synthesis. Why selective for lymphocytes: most cells can use BOTH de novo and salvage pathways for GMP synthesis. Lymphocytes (T and B cells) are HIGHLY DEPENDENT on the de novo pathway (limited salvage activity) → IMPDH inhibition selectively depletes guanine nucleotides in lymphocytes → impaired DNA synthesis → impaired proliferation → immunosuppression. Two IMPDH isoforms: type I (constitutive, most cells) and type II (inducible, upregulated in activated lymphocytes — MPA preferentially inhibits type II). Used in: transplant rejection prevention, lupus nephritis. Reference: Harper's — MMF/IMPDH.

questions.questionText: Thymidylate synthase (TS) catalyzes the conversion of dUMP to dTMP. The source of both the one-carbon unit AND reducing equivalents in this reaction is:
questions.optionB: N5,N10-methylene-THF (which is oxidized to DHF in the process — the only folate reaction where THF is NOT regenerated directly)
questions.explanation: Thymidylate synthase reaction — unique biochemistry: dUMP + N5,N10-methylene-THF → dTMP + DHF. This reaction is UNIQUE among folate-dependent reactions because: (1) N5,N10-methylene-THF acts as BOTH the one-carbon donor AND the reducing agent (provides methyl group + 2 hydrogens), (2) THF is oxidized to DHF (all other folate reactions regenerate THF directly), (3) Therefore, DHFR (dihydrofolate reductase) is ESSENTIAL to regenerate THF from DHF for continued thymidylate synthesis. This explains why DHFR inhibitors (methotrexate, trimethoprim) and TS inhibitors (5-FU → FdUMP) are synergistic — both block thymidylate availability → 'thymineless death' of rapidly dividing cells. FdUMP forms a stable ternary complex with TS and N5,N10-methylene-THF (suicide inhibition). Reference: Harper's — Thymidylate Synthase.

questions.questionText: Cladribine (2-chlorodeoxyadenosine/2-CdA) is effective against hairy cell leukemia because it:
questions.optionB: Is resistant to ADA degradation → accumulates as 2-CdATP in cells with high dCK activity (lymphocytes) → inhibits DNA repair and induces apoptosis even in non-dividing cells
questions.optionC: Blocks purine ring synthesis at the first step
questions.explanation: Cladribine — purine analog pharmacology: 2-chlorodeoxyadenosine is an adenosine analog with a chlorine at position 2 of the purine ring making it RESISTANT to deamination by ADA. Phosphorylated by deoxycytidine kinase (dCK) → 2-CdAMP → 2-CdADP → 2-CdATP (active form). 2-CdATP accumulates preferentially in lymphocytes (high dCK:5'-nucleotidase ratio). Effects: (1) Incorporated into DNA → single-strand breaks, (2) Activates caspases → apoptosis, (3) Inhibits DNA repair (PARP, DNA ligase), (4) Uniquely cytotoxic to BOTH dividing AND non-dividing cells (most antimetabolites only kill dividing cells). This is why it works in hairy cell leukemia (slowly dividing tumor). Achieves >90% complete remission with single course. Also used in CLL, MS. Reference: Harper's — Cladribine.

questions.questionText: The de novo synthesis of purine nucleotides differs from pyrimidine synthesis in that:
questions.optionA: Purines are synthesized from preformed bases
questions.optionB: The purine ring is assembled stepwise ON the ribose-5-phosphate backbone (IMP built on PRPP), while the pyrimidine ring is synthesized FIRST as a free base (orotate) and then attached to ribose-5-phosphate
questions.optionC: Pyrimidines require more ATP than purines
questions.explanation: De novo purine vs pyrimidine synthesis — structural comparison: PURINE de novo: ring built stepwise ON ribose-5-phosphate. PRPP + glutamine → PRA (PRPP amidotransferase, rate-limiting, committed step) → IMP (10 steps total). Ring contributors: glycine (C4, C5, N7), glutamine (N3, N9), aspartate (N1), CO2 (C6), N10-formyl-THF (C2, C8). IMP → AMP or GMP. PYRIMIDINE de novo: ring synthesized FIRST as free base, THEN attached to ribose-5-phosphate. CO2 + glutamine → carbamoyl phosphate (CPS-II, rate-limiting) → + aspartate → dihydroorotate → orotate → OMP (orotate + PRPP by orotate phosphoribosyltransferase) → UMP. Key: CPS-II (cytoplasmic, uses glutamine) vs CPS-I (mitochondrial, uses NH3 + NAG, urea cycle). Reference: Harper's — Purine vs Pyrimidine Synthesis.
questions.conceptTag: Purine vs pyrimidine de novo synthesis comparison
